title: Faceting and Saving Plots
description: Create multi-panel plots with faceting and save your plots to files.

slides.title: Faceting: Small Multiples
slides.content:
Faceting splits your plot into panels based on a categorical variable. Two functions:

- `facet_wrap(~var)` – wraps panels into a grid
- `facet_grid(rows ~ cols)` – creates a 2D grid

```r
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~ cyl)
```

slides.title: facet_wrap() Example
slides.codeSnippet:
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~ cyl, nrow = 1)

quizData.question: Which function creates a 2D grid of panels based on two variables?
quizData.explanation: facet_grid(rows ~ cols) creates a 2D panel layout.

slides.title: Saving Plots with ggsave()
slides.content:
After creating a plot, you can save it to a file using `ggsave()`.

```r
ggsave("myplot.png", width = 6, height = 4, dpi = 300)
```

By default, it saves the last plot. You can also pass a plot object.

Supported formats: png, pdf, jpeg, svg, etc.

slides.title: Save a Plot
slides.content: Run this in your R session (will save to working directory):
slides.codeSnippet:
library(ggplot2)
p <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) + geom_point()
ggsave("mtcars_scatter.png", plot = p, width = 5, height = 4)

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: Use `ggsave()` with appropriate `dpi` for publications (300 dpi) and `width/height` in inches.
